lines: // CSV spec for IN and NOT IN subqueries
-// KNN function combined with IN/NOT IN subquery
-// if there are null on the left hand side of IN or IN subquery, the row will be filtered out regardless of the right hand side value.
| WHERE gender IN (FROM employees | WHERE emp_no IN (10001, 10002, 10010) | KEEP gender)
-// if there are null on the right hand side of NOT IN or NOT IN subquery, the row will be filtered out regardless of the left hand side value.
| WHERE languages NOT IN (FROM employees | WHERE emp_no IN (10001, 10003, 10005, 10006, 10020) | KEEP languages)
-// -- complex disjunctive IN/NOT IN subqueries mixed with AND/OR and other predicates --
-// These exercise the LeftSemiJoin rewrite for InSubquery expressions that are nested under
-// arbitrary boolean shapes (AND inside OR, OR inside AND, NOT, multi-way OR). The plan stacks
-// one LeftSemiJoin per InSubquery and lets the standard expression engine evaluate the
-// surrounding AND/OR/NOT — including SQL three-valued logic when NULLs are involved.
-// (IN sub1 AND salary > 60000) OR (NOT IN sub2 AND gender == "F")
-// Branch A picks rows whose emp_no is in the sub1 set (languages == 1) AND salary > 60000.
-// Branch B picks rows not in sub2 (salary > 50000) AND gender == "F".
-// (IN sub1 OR languages == 1) AND (NOT IN sub2 OR salary > 65000)
-// Conjunction of two disjunctions, each containing an IN/NOT IN subquery — the LeftSemiJoin
-// approach evaluates each mark separately and the outer AND/OR machinery composes the result.
-// emp_no <= 10020 AND NOT (emp_no IN sub1 AND salary > 60000)
-// NOT applied to a conjunction containing an IN subquery; combined with a normal predicate.
-// The IN subquery inside NOT(...) lifts to a LeftSemiJoin and NOT is evaluated by the engine.
-// emp_no IN sub1 OR (gender == "F" AND (salary > 70000 OR emp_no NOT IN sub2))
-// Three boolean operators above the second InSubquery (OR > AND > OR > NOT IN); each of the
-// two subqueries becomes its own LeftSemiJoin and the resulting expression is evaluated as-is.
-// (IN sub1 AND gender == "M") OR (IN sub2 AND salary > 70000) OR (NOT IN sub3 AND gender == "F")
-// Three disjuncts, each containing an IN/NOT IN subquery wrapped in AND with an extra predicate.
-// Produces three LeftSemiJoin nodes stacked below a single Filter that ORs the three marks.
-// IN / NOT IN subqueries whose source command is ROW.
-// All gated on the `subquery_with_row` capability, which is dev-only.
-// Plain ROW as the IN-subquery source: the right side is a single scalar
-// position carrying the literal value, so the IN check matches exactly the
-// one employee whose emp_no equals that value.
-// NOT IN with a single-value ROW: the right side is a non-null literal, so
-// ANTI semantics keep every row except the one matching the ROW value.
-// WHERE filter inside the ROW subquery is satisfied by the literal, so the
-// row survives and the outer IN behaves like the basic case.
| WHERE emp_no IN (ROW x = 10001 | WHERE x > 0)
-// WHERE filter inside the ROW subquery is unsatisfiable for the literal,
-// so the ROW produces no rows and the IN-subquery output is empty -> no
| WHERE emp_no IN (ROW x = 10001 | WHERE x > 99999)
-// EVAL inside the ROW subquery folds the literal expression to 10001,
-// which is then used as the right-side IN value.
| WHERE emp_no IN (ROW x = 1 | EVAL x = x + 10000)
-// Several processing commands chained inside the ROW subquery: the ROW
-// declares two fields, EVAL rewrites one to a matching employee id, KEEP
-// trims the projection down to that single column, and LIMIT caps the
-// (already single) row count. The IN-subquery sees a single-column,
-// Multi-field ROW projected down to one column via KEEP. The KEEP guarantees
-// the IN-subquery's single-column requirement; the dropped `y` is invisible
| WHERE emp_no IN (ROW x = 10001, y = 10002 | KEEP x)
-// Multivalue ROW followed by MV_EXPAND: the single multivalue position is
-// expanded into three single-value rows before the SemiJoin sees the right
-// side, so each value contributes a usable match key. Without MV_EXPAND the
-// multivalue position would be canonicalized to NULL on the right and no
| WHERE emp_no IN (ROW x = [10001, 10002, 10003] | MV_EXPAND x)
-// Multiple ROW subqueries fanned out through a FROM. Each ROW declares the
-// same column name and type so the UnionAll output keeps a single emp_no
-// column carrying all three synthesised values.
-// Multiple ROW subqueries each carrying its own inner pipeline. The first
-// branch's EVAL rewrites emp_no to 10001; the second branch's WHERE is
-// satisfied by the literal and keeps 10002. Both branches union into a
| WHERE emp_no IN (FROM (ROW emp_no = 1 | EVAL emp_no = emp_no + 10000),
-// Mix of an index pattern and a ROW subquery inside the IN-subquery body:
-// the union of employees and the synthesised ROW is filtered down to
-// {10001, 99999}; only the value that exists in employees produces a match.
-// Mix of a FROM subquery and a ROW subquery inside the IN-subquery body.
-// The FROM subquery contributes the 8 high-salary employees, the ROW adds
-// the synthetic 99999 (which matches no employee); the outer IN therefore
| WHERE emp_no IN (FROM (FROM employees | WHERE salary > 70000),
-// All three FROM-source flavours combined inside the IN-subquery body: an
-// index pattern, a FROM subquery, and a ROW subquery. The outer WHERE on
-// the union narrows the right-side to {10097..10100, 99999}; only the four
-// employees whose emp_no falls in that range survive.
-// Processing command (STATS) attached to the outer query after the IN
-// subquery with ROW source: the IN matches exactly one row, so the count
-// EVAL after the IN-subquery applies to the matching outer rows. The IN
-// keeps three employees and the outer EVAL derives a `decade` value for
-// each that is then projected alongside emp_no.
-// IN subquery with ROW source AND-ed with a regular predicate. The ROW's
-// MV_EXPAND produces three candidate ids; the AND with `gender == "M"`
-// further restricts the output to male employees only.
| WHERE emp_no IN (ROW x = [10001, 10002, 10003] | MV_EXPAND x)
-// IN subquery with ROW source OR-ed with a regular predicate. The ROW
-// adds emp_no=10001 to the set otherwise selected by `salary > 74000`,
-// which exercises the LeftSemiJoin rewrite that handles InSubquery under
| WHERE emp_no IN (ROW x = 10001) OR salary > 74000
-// Nested IN-subquery where the inner WHERE-IN also uses a ROW as its
-// source. The inner ROW provides a literal x=1 that is used to filter
-// _index of every employees row is "employees", and the subquery emits
-// "employees" for every row, so the IN check is satisfied by all rows.
-    // ---- multi-valued left-hand-side of disjunctive IN subquery (LeftSemiJoin) ----
+    // ---- multi-valued left-hand-side of disjunctive IN subquery (MarkJoin) ----
// For an {@code IN (subquery)} that is nested under {@code OR} (so the rewrite produces a
// operator must produce a boolean mark attribute that captures the three-valued
// result of {@code leftField IN (subquery)} for every left row.
// For a multi-valued left key, the SQL/ES|QL convention used by the {@code In} operator is to
// emit {@code NULL} and a "single-value function encountered multi-value" warning. The
-    // {@code LeftSemiJoin} filter-path inherits that semantics because it materializes the mark
+    // {@code MarkJoin} filter-path inherits that semantics because it materializes the mark
// through the same {@code In} evaluator. The hash-join path, however, builds the mark from a
// sentinel attribute populated by a LEFT join and an {@code IsNull(leftField)} check — neither
// of which models the single-value-function/MV warning. As a result the two paths can disagree
// exercise both paths so the divergence is observable from the integration layer.
-     * Disjunctive IN subquery (LeftSemiJoin) over a multi-valued left key on the filter path.
+     * Disjunctive IN subquery (MarkJoin) over a multi-valued left key on the filter path.
* Indexes a row with {@code color=["red","green"]} alongside the existing single-valued rows,
* then runs {@code color IN (subq) OR id > N} for two values of {@code N} so the OR's
* non-IN side is, respectively, {@code FALSE} and {@code TRUE} for the multi-valued row.
* Same query as {@link #testDisjunctiveInSubqueryMultiValueLeftKeyFilterPath} but with the
* hash-join threshold pinned to 0 so the rewrite always takes the hash-join path. Both paths
* must agree on the set of surviving rows; any divergence indicates that the hash-join
-     * implementation of {@code LeftSemiJoin} fails to honor the {@code In} operator's MV
+     * implementation of {@code MarkJoin} fails to honor the {@code In} operator's MV
* Currently fails because the hash-join path's mark expression (CASE based on
-     * Top-level conjunctive IN / NOT IN (SEMI / ANTI {@code SemiJoin}, not {@code LeftSemiJoin})
+     * Top-level conjunctive IN / NOT IN (SEMI / ANTI {@code SemiJoin}, not {@code MarkJoin})
* over a multi-valued left key. The filter path materializes {@code Filter(In(...))} /
* {@code Filter(Not(In(...)))} which, by {@code In}'s three-valued semantics, drops MV-LHS
* rows (mark=NULL → treated as FALSE under WHERE). The hash-join path is forced via
// MV-derived) into its reserved group 0, surfacing as a single NULL position at index 0 of
// the dedup output. The post-dedup logic derives {@code rightHadNulls} from that position
// and the short-circuit decisions (ANTI: any NULL → empty; SEMI: only all-NULL → empty;
-    // LEFT_SEMI: all-NULL → mark=NULL). The tests below pin that contract end-to-end and
+    // MARK: all-NULL → mark=NULL). The tests below pin that contract end-to-end and
// ensure filter and hash-join paths agree.
-     * LeftSemiJoin}) with a multi-valued row in the subquery output. The MV row canonicalizes to
+     * MarkJoin}) with a multi-valued row in the subquery output. The MV row canonicalizes to
* NULL on the right, so {@code In}'s three-valued mark is {@code NULL} for left rows whose
* key doesn't match any SV right value. Combined with the outer OR clause, this surfaces
* the {@code NULL OR TRUE = TRUE} vs {@code NULL OR FALSE = NULL} distinction — and both
-                    "LeftSemiJoin filter and hash-join paths disagree on MV-on-right for query:\n" + query,
+                    "MarkJoin filter and hash-join paths disagree on MV-on-right for query:\n" + query,
JoinConfig joinConfig = new JoinConfig(semiJoin.config().type(), leftKeys, rightFields, semiJoin.config().joinOnConditions());
-                return new LeftSemiJoin(leftSemiJoin.source(), leftSemiJoin.left(), right, joinConfig, leftSemiJoin.markAttribute());
+                return new MarkJoin(markJoin.source(), markJoin.left(), right, joinConfig, markJoin.markAttribute());
* Resolves {@link InSubquery} expressions in {@link Filter} conditions by rewriting them into
- * {@link SemiJoin}, {@link AntiJoin}, or {@link LeftSemiJoin} nodes depending on where the
+ * {@link SemiJoin}, {@link AntiJoin}, or {@link MarkJoin} nodes depending on where the
* {@link InSubquery} sits inside the boolean expression:
*   <li>An {@code InSubquery} (optionally wrapped in {@link Not}) at the top of an AND-conjunct
*       becomes a row-filtering {@link SemiJoin} / {@link AntiJoin} stacked on top of the
* remaining filter — the most efficient shape, used for the common conjunctive case.
*   <li>An {@code InSubquery} that appears as a child of {@link Or} (or of {@link Not} below an
- *       {@link Or}) is replaced with a synthetic boolean attribute and a {@link LeftSemiJoin}
+ *       {@link Or}) is replaced with a synthetic boolean attribute and a {@link MarkJoin}
*       is stacked below the rewritten {@link Filter}; the mark attribute carries the
* three-valued {@code IN} result up into normal boolean evaluation.
*   <li>An {@code InSubquery} wrapped in any other expression (a function argument, an
* the pre-resolution plan because by the time this method returns the originating
* {@link InSubquery} expressions have been replaced with
-     * {@link SemiJoin}/{@link AntiJoin}/{@link LeftSemiJoin} and are no longer visible to plan
-     * traversals. The {@code WHERE} counter still picks up SemiJoin/AntiJoin/LeftSemiJoin in the
+     * {@link SemiJoin}/{@link AntiJoin}/{@link MarkJoin} and are no longer visible to plan
+     * traversals. The {@code WHERE} counter still picks up SemiJoin/AntiJoin/MarkJoin in the
* post-resolution plan walk (see {@code FeatureMetric#WHERE}), so the {@code WHERE} bit does
* not need to be set up-front here.
private record SemiOrAntiJoinSpec(Source source, LogicalPlan subquery, JoinConfig config, boolean anti) {}
-     * Spec for a {@link LeftSemiJoin} stacked below the remaining filter for an {@link InSubquery}
+     * Spec for a {@link MarkJoin} stacked below the remaining filter for an {@link InSubquery}
* that appears under {@code OR}/{@code NOT}/{@code AND} but not as a top-level AND conjunct.
* The mark attribute is referenced from the rewritten boolean expression.
-    private record LeftSemiJoinSpec(Source source, LogicalPlan subquery, JoinConfig config, Attribute markAttribute) {}
+    private record MarkJoinSpec(Source source, LogicalPlan subquery, JoinConfig config, Attribute markAttribute) {}
// satisfy the original IN/NOT IN predicate; they are correct only when the predicate is
-        // Joins applied BEFORE the remaining filter. LeftSemiJoins emit a boolean mark attribute
+        // Joins applied BEFORE the remaining filter. MarkJoins emit a boolean mark attribute
// referenced from the rewritten remaining condition; the mark carries the three-valued
// IN result through the normal boolean evaluation in the surrounding OR/AND/NOT shape.
// Synthetic Eval aliases for constant left-hand side expressions (e.g. WHERE 10001 IN (subquery)).
// Materialized as an Eval below the joins; the synthetic attributes are projected away above.
// Either no InSubquery in the conjunct (passes through unchanged), or InSubquery is
-            // nested inside OR (rewritten with LeftSemiJoin), or InSubquery sits under a
+            // nested inside OR (rewritten with MarkJoin), or InSubquery sits under a
// non-boolean wrapper (left as-is for {@link #verify} to reject).
-        // Stack LeftSemiJoins first — they are applied before the remaining filter so the mark
+        // Stack MarkJoins first — they are applied before the remaining filter so the mark
// attributes are available to the rewritten boolean expression.
-            current = new LeftSemiJoin(ls.source, current, ls.subquery, ls.config, ls.markAttribute);
+            current = new MarkJoin(mj.source, current, mj.subquery, mj.config, mj.markAttribute);
-        // Apply remaining filter conditions on top of LeftSemiJoins (so mark attributes are in scope).
+        // Apply remaining filter conditions on top of MarkJoins (so mark attributes are in scope).
-        // The mark attributes from LeftSemiJoins (and any synthetic constant Eval columns introduced
+        // The mark attributes from MarkJoins (and any synthetic constant Eval columns introduced
// for foldable LHS) are flagged synthetic so the analyzer's default output projection
// (planWithoutSyntheticAttributes) drops them — preserving the filter's apparent schema.
* Walks the boolean expression replacing every {@link InSubquery} reachable through
* {@link And}/{@link Or}/{@link Not} (i.e. boolean position) with a fresh synthetic mark
-     * attribute, recording a {@link LeftSemiJoinSpec} per replacement. {@link InSubquery}
+     * attribute, recording a {@link MarkJoinSpec} per replacement. {@link InSubquery}
* occurrences that sit under a non-boolean wrapper (function argument, comparison, etc.) are
* left in place for {@link #verify} to reject. Any expression with no eligible
* {@link InSubquery} below it is returned unchanged.
-    private static Expression rewriteOrContextInSubqueries(Expression expr, List<LeftSemiJoinSpec> joins, List<Alias> syntheticEvals) {
+    private static Expression rewriteOrContextInSubqueries(Expression expr, List<MarkJoinSpec> joins, List<Alias> syntheticEvals) {
@@ -254,7 +254,7 @@ private static Expression rewriteOrContextInSubqueries(Expression expr, List<Lef
return child == not.field() ? not : new Not(not.source(), child);
